summary:
**Claim**: Gluteal claudication is the most common symptom of iliac occlusive disease.  
**Evidence**: - Iliac arteries supply the gluteal muscles, which are extensively engaged during ambulation. - Unlike calf claudication, gluteal claudicipation occurs due to reduced perfusion in high-demand muscle groups directly affected by iliac artery obstruction.  
**Answer**: B. Gluteal claudication.  

This conclusion follows from anatomical and physiological principles: when iliac arteries become occluded, blood flow to the gluteal region diminishes significantly during physical activity, leading to exercise-induced leg pain specifically in the buttocks. Calf claudication, though common in peripheral arterial disease overall, arises primarily from femoral or popliteal involvement and is less typical in isolated iliac disease. The high functional demand placed on gluteal muscles makes them uniquely susceptible to ischemia in this context, explaining why symptoms manifest there first and most prominently. This distinction underscores the importance of localizing symptoms to identify underlying vascular pathology accurately. (498 tokens)

This conclusion follows from anatomical and physiological principles: when iliac arteries become occluded, blood flow to the gluteal region diminishes significantly during physical activity, leading to exercise-induced leg pain specifically in the buttocks. Calf claudication, though common in peripheral arterial disease overall, arises primarily from femoral or popliteal involvement and is less typical in isolated iliac disease. The high functional demand placed on gluteal muscles makes them uniquely susceptible to ischemia in this context, explaining why symptoms manifest there first and most prominently. This distinction underscores the importance of localizing symptoms to identify underlying vascular pathology accurately. (498 tokens). Answer choice: A
